name: Doppler Secrets Manager
what_it_does: Cloud-based secrets manager that syncs environment variables and secrets across teams, environments, and cloud providers, with a REST API, CLI, and MCP server for agent access.
use_cases: Fetching secrets and environment variables for agent workflows without hardcoding credentials
Syncing secrets across dev/staging/prod environments with automatic propagation
Rotating secrets and pushing updated values to downstream integrations (AWS, Vercel, etc.)
Giving AI agents read-only access to specific project secrets via scoped service tokens
Auditing secret access history across automated workflows
not_for: Certificate and PKI management (use Vault or cloud KMS)
Runtime secret injection at the OS or HSM level
Orgs requiring fully self-hosted secrets infrastructure with no SaaS dependency
Large-scale encryption-as-a-service (use Vault Transit or AWS KMS)
best_when: You want developer-friendly, SaaS-hosted secrets management with native integrations to cloud platforms and CI/CD tools, and want agents to access secrets via a clean API or MCP server.
avoid_when: Your organization requires fully self-hosted secrets management or you already have HashiCorp Vault deployed and working.

auth.notes: Personal tokens for full access; service tokens are scoped to a single project/config with read-only or read-write permissions. Service tokens are ideal for agent workflows — narrow scope, no user access.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Free tier: up to 5 users, unlimited secrets in 1 project
pricing.paid_tiers: Team plan from $6/user/month; Enterprise with audit logs and SSO
pricing.notes: Free tier is genuinely useful for small teams and solo developers. Service token access is available on all plans including free.

performance.rate_limits: API rate limits not publicly documented; service-tier dependent

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: REST API returns standard HTTP status codes with descriptive JSON error messages. Service token scope violations return 403 with clear context.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Secret updates are idempotent — setting the same key/value twice is safe. Secret downloads are read-only and fully idempotent.
known_agent_gotchas: Service tokens are config-scoped — agents must use the correct token for each project/environment combination
Secret download returns all secrets at once (no per-key fetch) — agents receive the full config, must extract what they need
Secret changes require a Doppler 'release' concept in some workflows — understand when immediate propagation is needed
MCP server is community/official but newer — test thoroughly before relying on it in production agent pipelines
Webhooks fire on secret changes but require HTTPS endpoint — local development needs a tunnel
